channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump’s Blockade Risks Upending an Emerging Détente With China
item.description: In a thinly veiled critique of the war in Iran, China’s leader said the world could not risk reverting “to the law of the jungle.”
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger and Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: President Trump meeting with China’s leader, Xi Jinping, in South Korea last October. They will meet again next month.

item.title: Rubio Hosts Israel and Lebanon for Rare Meeting Shadowed by U.S.-Iran War
item.description: The gathering ended with encouraging words, even as Israel continued to refuse to halt its military campaign against Iran-backed Hezbollah in Lebanon.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley and Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio meeting with officials from Lebanon and Israel at the State Department in Washington on Tuesday.

item.title: How China’s Weapons Transfers to Iran Have Evolved Over Decades
item.description: China has denied claims this month by U.S. intelligence that it might have shipped arms to Iran. In recent years, China has supported Iran with dual-use parts.
item.dc:creator: David Pierson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Military delegates at an annual legislative session in Beijing last month.

item.title: Vance Says Pope Leo Should Be More Careful When Talking About Theology
item.description: The vice president, who is Catholic, took issue with Pope Leo XIV’s statement that disciples of Christ are “never on the side of those who once wielded the sword and today drop bombs.”
item.dc:creator: Anton Troianovski
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vice President JD Vance spoke at a Turning Point USA event in Athens, Ga., on Tuesday.

item.title: Pope Leo’s Challenge in Cameroon: Show African Catholics How Much They Matter
item.description: Catholicism is growing fast on the continent, yet Africans play a comparatively small role in church leadership. Cameroon, which Leo XIV will visit Wednesday, shows the divide.
item.dc:creator: Motoko Rich
item.media:description: Posing after Mass on Sunday at St. Joseph Metropolitan Cathedral in Bamenda, Cameroon. For some Catholics in the country, a visit by Pope Leo XIV on Wednesday will be a reminder of disparities in the church.

item.title: Gallego Dismissed Rumors of a ‘Flirty’ Swalwell, Highlighting a Culture of Silence
item.description: The admission by Senator Ruben Gallego that he had heard, but disbelieved, rumors about Eric Swalwell and women showed the attitude on Capitol Hill toward men accused of behaving badly.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.category: House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Ruben Gallego at the Capitol on Tuesday. He spent considerable time with Mr. Swalwell and chaired his short-lived 2020 presidential campaign.

item.title: A New Accuser Says Eric Swalwell Sexually Assaulted Her
item.description: The woman said Mr. Swalwell, who resigned from Congress on Tuesday afternoon, raped her in a West Hollywood hotel room in 2018. She said she believed she was drugged.
item.dc:creator: Jill Cowan and Madison Malone Kircher
item.media:credit: Ariana Drehsler for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lonna Drewes, left, said she had waited years to speak, because of “fear, not doubt.”

item.title: Swalwell’s Exit Injects ‘Chaos’ Into California Governor’s Race
item.description: Democratic candidates see sudden voter interest in the sleepy contest as a campaign opportunity. All are scrambling for support from former backers of Eric Swalwell.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Rosenhall and Theodore Schleifer
item.media:description: Eric Swalwell’s departure seemed most likely to benefit the two Democrats who had been polling closest to him.

item.title: Resignations of Eric Swalwell and Tony Gonzales Set Up Special Election Fights in Texas and California
item.description: Gov. Gavin Newsom of California called a special election to replace Mr. Swalwell, while Gov. Greg Abbott of Texas had yet to announce one for Mr. Gonzales’s successor.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman and Laurel Rosenhall

item.title: Prosecutors Make Surprise Visit to Fed as Pirro Defends Investigation
item.description: The Justice Department’s criminal inquiry into the Federal Reserve threatens to delay the confirmation of the next chair.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith and Glenn Thrush
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: An overview showing construction at the Federal Reserve last year. The $2.5 billion renovation project is at the center of an investigation into the central bank.

item.title: King Charles III and Queen Camilla Will Visit D.C., New York and Virginia
item.description: The planned four-day state visit comes at a fraught time in the U.S.-U.K. relationship, following President Trump’s frequent belittling of the British prime minister.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
Camilla, Queen Consort of the United Kingdom
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Queen Camilla and King Charles III of Britain earlier this month.

item.title: Justice Dept. Moves to Vacate Jan. 6 Convictions for Far-Right Extremists
item.description: Defending the convictions would likely have required administration officials to assert that far-right groups were acting on behalf of President Trump on Jan. 6, 2021.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:description: President Trump released Stewart Rhodes, the founder and leader of the Oath Keepers, from prison but did not grant him a full pardon.

item.title: JD Vance Heckled In Antiwar Protest at Turning Point USA Event
item.description: Vice President JD Vance appeared to express sympathy with critics of the war with Iran: “I recognize that young voters do not love the policy we have in the Middle East, OK. I understand.”
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Mr. Vance, right, addressed the audience outbursts Tuesday, agreeing that “Jesus Christ does not support genocide” and defending the Trump administration’s Middle East policies

item.title: The $40 Half Chicken That Ruffled Brooklyn
item.description: A New York City councilman’s Instagram post is just the latest entry in a fierce debate about the price of dining out.
item.dc:creator: Luke Fortney
item.media:credit: Cole Saladino for The New York Times
item.media:description: It’s half a rotisserie chicken. What could it cost?

item.title: War Exposes Gaps in India’s Clean Energy Push
item.description: The country’s creaking electricity grid is lagging behind its push for renewable sources — just as its growing economy hungers for oil stranded by war.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli and Suhasini Raj
item.media:credit: Saumya Khandelwal for The New York Times
item.media:description: A floating solar farm in Visakhapatnam, India.

item.title: A Police Chief Had a $4.5 Million Gambling Problem. No One Knew.
item.description: New Haven’s police chief, Karl Jacobson, resigned abruptly after his deputies saw red flags, including missing money. He has pleaded not guilty to embezzling city money to gamble on sports.
item.dc:creator: Hurubie Meko
item.media:credit: City of New Haven, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Karl Jacobson served as New Haven’s police chief for three years before his career unraveled.

item.title: Hampshire College Will Close Amid Student Enrollment Declines
item.description: Other small private colleges like Hampshire have closed in recent years as financial pressures and competition for students increase.
item.dc:creator: Mark Arsenault
item.media:credit: David L. Ryan/The Boston Globe, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Hampshire College’s campus in Amherst, Mass.

item.title: Texas A&M Picks an Insider for President After Months of Conflict
item.description: Debates over how to teach about gender, sexuality and other topics have shaken the school, and lead to the ouster of the previous president at the College Station campus last summer.
item.dc:creator: Vimal Patel
item.media:credit: Annie Mulligan for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Texas A&M campus in College Station.

item.title: How Peter Magyar Defeated Viktor Orban, a Former Ally, In Hungary’s Election
item.description: For years, Peter Magyar was a loyal ally of Viktor Orban, the far-right Hungarian leader. Then he changed sides — and defeated his former boss in a landslide victory on Sunday. Does he represent real change?
item.dc:creator: Andrew Higgins and Lili Rutai
item.media:credit: Akos Stiller for The New York Times
item.media:description: Peter Magyar campaigning in 2024 in Viktor Orban’s hometown, Felcsut.

item.title: Hochul Proposes Tax on N.Y.C. Second Homes That Are Worth $5 Million
item.description: Gov. Kathy Hochul, who has opposed raising some taxes, favored a “pied-à-terre” luxury tax because it largely targets the ultrawealthy who primarily live outside New York City.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Oreskes
item.media:credit: Liam Kennedy for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ms. Hochul repeatedly expressed her unwavering opposition to Mr. Mamdani’s tax proposals.

item.title: Camp Mystic Hearing in Texas Weighs Reopening After Deadly Flooding
item.description: Testimony at a hearing this week has focused on what camp leaders knew and did as floodwaters rose in July, killing at least 116 people.
item.dc:creator: Jesus Jiménez
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: Generations of girls have spent their summers at Camp Mystic, nestled along the Guadalupe River in the Texas Hill Country.

item.title: Ricky Cobb Has Built a Mini-Empire Through ’70s Nostalgia
item.description: Ricky Cobb has built a big online following with his irreverent postings about the absurdities of the 1970s.
item.dc:creator: Joe Drape
item.media:credit: Lyndon French for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ricky Cobb’s fans say he celebrates an era in American life that seems less uptight and more fun.

item.title: Pope Leo to Trump: ‘I Have No Fear’
item.description: A harsh attack by the president drew a quick response from the pontiff, a reminder of his uniquely moral voice.
item.dc:creator: Daniel J. Wakin
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: The World Has Forgotten Sudan, but I Haven’t
item.description: The spirit of the Sudanese people cannot be crushed.
item.dc:creator: Mohammed Ahmed

item.title: Have Mental Health Concerns Influenced Your Choice to Have Children?
item.description: If you are on the fence, or have already made a decision, we want to hear about it.
item.dc:creator: Christina Caron

item.title: Late Night Laughs at Explanations of Trump’s Jesus ‘Joke’
item.description: Ronny Chieng poked fun at JD Vance’s defense of President Trump posting an image of himself as a Christ-like figure.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Billy Crystal to Return to Broadway With Solo Show ’860’ About Losing His Home in L.A. Fires
item.description: The actor-comedian said he will return to Broadway this fall with a new solo show called “860,” named for the address of his destroyed family home.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:description: Billy Crystal at this year’s Academy Awards ceremony in March.

item.title: Surgeon Who Removed Wrong Organ From Patient Is Charged in His Death
item.description: Dr. Thomas Shaknovsky tried to persuade his colleagues in the operating room that the liver he removed from a 70-year-old patient was a spleen, according to Florida’s Health Department.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:description: William and Beverly Bryan.

item.title: Inside the Radical Zionist Group Linked to an N.Y.C. Assassination Plot
item.description: The Jewish Defense League had long been largely inactive. But an arrest in a plan to kill a Palestinian activist shed light on an apparent resurgence of far-right Zionism, inspired by the J.D.L.
item.dc:creator: Liam Stack
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Yisrael Yaacob Ben Avraham, the founder of J.D.L. 613, is an admirer of Meir Kahane, who created the original Jewish Defense League.

item.title: N.F.L. Reporter Resigns From The Athletic Amid an Investigation
item.description: The publication, which is owned by The New York Times, was investigating the conduct of Dianna Russini after photographs showed her with the head coach of the New England Patriots.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:description: Dianna Russini joined The Athletic in 2023 and was a co-host of the “Scoop City” podcast.
